sourceMovie.title: 28 Weeks Later
sourceMovie.whyPeopleLoveIt: People revisit 28 Weeks Later for the same reason it broke through in 2007: clear stakes, memorable faces, and a thriller rhythm that doesn't stall in the middle. Juan Carlos Fresnadillo keeps the ensemble pointed in one direction. The horror and thriller blend is the engine: it lets the film shift registers without feeling like two different movies stitched together.

recommendations.title: World War Z
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: The horror appeal of 28 Weeks Later — that dark edge, the thriller — translates cleanly into World War Z (2013). Different setting, same essential satisfaction.

recommendations.title: 28 Days Later
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: The filmmakers behind 28 Days Later (2002) clearly understood what makes horror work — the same quality that keeps 28 Weeks Later rewatchable. Expect the same dark rhythm, executed with a fresh angle.

recommendations.title: Night of the Living Dead
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Night of the Living Dead (1968) is the kind of film 28 Weeks Later fans discover and immediately send to their group chat. The horror overlap is real, but it's the dark emotional pull that seals the recommendation.

recommendations.title: 28 Years Later
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: 28 Years Later shares 28 Weeks Later's gift for horror — the 2025 film delivers thriller without overstaying its welcome. Consider it required viewing once you've finished rewatching your first pick.

recommendations.title: The Endless
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Much of what makes 28 Weeks Later compelling — the horror, the thriller — shows up in The Endless (2017) in a slightly different form. Both films understand that dark storytelling works best when it trusts the audience.

recommendations.title: Evil Dead Rise
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: The horror energy of 28 Weeks Later is alive in Evil Dead Rise (2023), filtered through a different story but the same emotional register. It earns its place on this list with a dark payoff that rivals the original pick.

recommendations.title: The Girl with All the Gifts
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: The Girl with All the Gifts (2016) operates in the same horror as 28 Weeks Later, but with its own distinct voice and pacing. The dark payoff will feel satisfyingly familiar without retreading the same ground.

recommendations.title: Hidden
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Hidden (2015) belongs to the same conversation as 28 Weeks Later — both anchor their horror in thriller emotion that doesn't let go. The dark undercurrent connects them more than any plot summary would suggest.

recommendations.title: Critters
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: If 28 Weeks Later's horror stayed with you, Critters (1986) delivers the same sensation through an entirely different story. The science-fiction through-line is the real common thread between them.

recommendations.title: Lights Out
recommendations.whyYoullLoveIt: Where 28 Weeks Later excels in horror, Lights Out (2016) doubles down on craft to comparable effect. The tonal similarities run deep enough that watching both back-to-back feels intentional.

faq.question: What should I watch after 28 Weeks Later?
faq.answer: Try World War Z, 28 Days Later, Night of the Living Dead—each shares DNA with 28 Weeks Later while changing the setting enough to feel fresh.

faq.question: What genre is 28 Weeks Later?
faq.answer: 28 Weeks Later (2007) is best described as Horror, Thriller, Science Fiction. Stores and algorithms use different labels, but that mix is what fans actually recognize on a rewatch.

faq.question: Is 28 Weeks Later part of a franchise?
faq.answer: Yes—it's part of the "28 Days/Weeks/Years Later Collection" storyline. You can enjoy it standalone, but sequels and tie-ins build on characters and continuity established here.
